title: Fix: Server shutdown does not call memory_service.close() — resources leaked
analysis:
The server's finally block in run_server() (main.py:222-224) only calls task_interceptor.drain(). It never invokes memory_service.close(), which properly shuts down DurableWriteQueue, GraphitiBackend, Mem0Backend, WriteJournal, and EventBuffer. Additionally, the reconciliation harness background loop task (created at line 161 via asyncio.create_task) is fire-and-forget — the task reference is discarded, so it can never be cancelled on shutdown.

MemoryService.close() (lines 95-108) already exists and properly closes all sub-resources with contextlib.suppress(Exception) guards. The fix is surgical: (1) store the harness loop task reference, (2) extract a _graceful_shutdown() helper for testability, (3) replace the bare drain() in the finally block with a full shutdown sequence.

The shutdown order must be: drain interceptor (flush pending commits/reconciliation) → cancel harness loop (stops background reconciliation + escalation server) → close memory service (backends + queues + journals) → close reconciliation journal (separate SQLite connection not managed by MemoryService). Each step is guarded against exceptions so subsequent steps still run.

There are no existing tests for run_server() or the shutdown path. The _graceful_shutdown helper makes this testable without mocking the entire server initialization chain.

steps.description: Write failing test: test_shutdown_calls_memory_service_close — create fused-memory/tests/test_server_shutdown.py, import the not-yet-existing _graceful_shutdown function from fused_memory.server.main, call it with an AsyncMock memory_service and None for other params, assert memory_service.close was awaited once. Test fails with ImportError since _graceful_shutdown does not exist yet.

Implement _graceful_shutdown(memory_service, task_interceptor, harness_loop_task, recon_journal) as a module-level async function in main.py. Initially: drain interceptor if present, then await memory_service.close(). Replace the finally block contents with: await _graceful_shutdown(memory_service, task_interceptor, harness_loop_task, recon_journal). Pre-initialize harness_loop_task=None and recon_journal=None before the try block. Step-1 test now passes.

Write failing test: test_shutdown_cancels_harness_loop_task — create a real asyncio.Task wrapping an infinite asyncio.sleep, pass it as harness_loop_task to _graceful_shutdown, assert the task is cancelled after shutdown completes. Test fails because _graceful_shutdown does not yet cancel the task.

Add harness loop task cancellation to _graceful_shutdown: if harness_loop_task is not None, cancel it, suppress CancelledError, and await it. In run_server(), store the return of asyncio.create_task(reconciliation_harness.run_loop()) into the harness_loop_task variable. Step-3 test now passes.

Write failing test: test_shutdown_resilient_to_drain_error — configure task_interceptor.drain() to raise RuntimeError, verify memory_service.close() is still called afterward. Test fails because current _graceful_shutdown lets the drain exception propagate and skip close().

Wrap each step in _graceful_shutdown with try/except Exception that logs the error and continues. This ensures memory_service.close() runs even if drain() or task cancellation fails. Step-5 test now passes.

Write failing test: test_shutdown_closes_reconciliation_journal — pass an AsyncMock recon_journal to _graceful_shutdown, verify its close() is awaited. Test fails because _graceful_shutdown does not yet close the journal.

Add recon_journal.close() as the final step in _graceful_shutdown (guarded with try/except). In run_server(), assign recon_journal=journal (line 124) when reconciliation is enabled, so it passes to _graceful_shutdown. Step-7 test now passes.

design_decisions.decision: Extract _graceful_shutdown() as a module-level async helper rather than inlining all logic in the finally block
design_decisions.rationale: run_server() is monolithic and untested. A standalone async function accepting its dependencies as parameters is directly testable with AsyncMock without mocking the entire server initialization chain. The finally block becomes a single line.

design_decisions.decision: Shutdown order: drain interceptor → cancel harness → close memory_service → close journal
design_decisions.rationale: drain() flushes pending targeted reconciliation and task file commits — these may write to backends, so backends must still be open. Harness cancellation triggers its own finally block (_stop_escalation_server). memory_service.close() shuts down backends + durable queue. ReconciliationJournal is closed last as it is a passive log.

design_decisions.decision: Each shutdown step is independently guarded with try/except + logging
design_decisions.rationale: The current code has a latent bug: if drain() raises, nothing else runs. Each cleanup step must be independent so a failure in one does not cascade and leak remaining resources.

design_decisions.decision: Include ReconciliationJournal.close() even though the task description only mentions MemoryService resources
design_decisions.rationale: ReconciliationJournal holds its own aiosqlite connection (journal.py:157-159) created at main.py:124-125. It is not managed by MemoryService.close(). Since we are fixing the shutdown path, closing this connection is a natural extension and prevents a SQLite connection leak.

design_decisions.decision: Do NOT cancel the _thread_monitor task explicitly
design_decisions.rationale: The thread monitor is a lightweight diagnostic loop with no external resources. When the event loop shuts down, all pending tasks are cancelled automatically. Adding explicit cancellation would add complexity for no resource-safety benefit.

reuse.how: Already exists and properly closes all sub-resources (durable_queue, graphiti, mem0, write_journal, event_buffer) with contextlib.suppress guards. Just needs to be called.
The harness loop's own finally block calls _stop_escalation_server(). Cancelling the task triggers this cleanup automatically — no need to duplicate escalation server shutdown logic.
Reuse the same AsyncMock + assert_called_once pattern for verifying close() calls in the new shutdown tests.
Already called in the finally block. Move into _graceful_shutdown as the first step, preserving existing behavior.
